title: Caramel corn
subtitle: Popcorn coated in a buttery caramel and baked until crisp
excerpt: Caramel corn is made by coating freshly popped corn in a warm butter-and-brown-sugar caramel, then baking it at a low temperature to set the coating hard and separate the kernels. The oven stage is what distinguishes caramel corn from a sticky stovetop version.
sourceNotes: Caramel popcorn is documented in American confectionery literature from the late nineteenth century; Cracker Jack, the commercial version, was introduced in 1896. The baked-caramel-corn method, which sets the coating in a low oven, is documented in public-domain American home-cooking literature from the early twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight container for up to 5 days. The caramel coating will soften if exposed to humidity; seal the container promptly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 120 degrees C; a low, slow bake sets the caramel without burning it

glossaryTerms.definition: Spreading caramel-coated popcorn on a tray and baking it at a low temperature (around 120 to 135 degrees C conventional) serves two purposes: it sets the caramel coating to a hard, snappy shell rather than a sticky one, and it separates the kernels from each other by allowing the coating to firm before they can stick together permanently. The tray must be stirred every 10 minutes during baking so that the kernels at the edges do not overbrown while the centre stays soft.

content.text: What separates caramel corn from popcorn dipped in caramel sauce is the
. Thirty minutes at low heat sets the coating crisp and keeps the kernels apart. Bicarbonate of soda stirred in at the end makes the caramel foam, which lightens the texture.
Pop the corn and make the caramel

Preheat the oven to 135 degrees C (conventional). Line two large baking trays with baking paper. Heat the oil in a large saucepan with a lid over medium-high heat. Add the popcorn kernels, put the lid on, and shake occasionally until the popping has largely stopped. Tip into the largest bowl you have; pick out any unpopped kernels. In the now-empty saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the brown sugar, golden syrup, and salt. Stir to combine, then boil without stirring for 4 minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in the vanilla and bicarbonate of soda; the mixture will foam. Pour immediately over the popped corn and toss quickly to coat.

Spread the coated popcorn in a single layer across the two lined trays. Bake for 30 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes to redistribute the coating and prevent sticking. The caramel will darken slightly and smell deeply of toffee. Remove from the oven and leave to cool completely on the trays, at least 20 minutes; the caramel sets hard as it cools. Break into pieces and store in an airtight container.
